behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is real filesystem watching behavior (recursive/subdirectory event propagation), which is OS- and watcher-backend-dependent; mocked events can hide real misses.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a real temp directory tree, create a nested subdirectory, write a file inside it, and assert exactly one transformed output appears for that nested file path.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is primarily deterministic path+time-window logic inside dedup state handling; real OS/file events are not required to expose the core bug.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the dedup component with a known window, submit the same file path twice at controlled timestamps (or mocked clock), and assert first is accepted and second is rejected; assert acceptance again after the window expires.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: The behavior explicitly depends on real OS event duplication/coalescing patterns; synthetic single-event mocks can miss duplicate-event races seen in production.
behaviors.test_description: Run the daemon with a real watched directory, perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple events (create + append/rename), capture processing invocations, and assert one output/one pipeline execution for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: This is an OS-level watcher lifecycle problem (inode/path invalidation and re-subscription) that only appears with real directory deletion/recreation semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Launch daemon on a real temp watch path, delete the watched directory, recreate it with the same name, then create a new file and verify it is processed without restarting the daemon.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: The main failure boundary is module handoff between intake, metadata lookup, and queue ordering. This can be caught by wiring these modules together with local filesystem metadata without requiring full OS watcher behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Wire intake+ordering+processing modules in-process, create multiple test files with controlled timestamps (or stubbed stat metadata at module boundary), enqueue them as simultaneous arrivals, and assert processing order matches timestamp ordering rules.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on real write timing and filesystem visibility (partial writes, size stabilization, locks/flush timing). Mocks usually hide these race conditions.
behaviors.test_description: Use a real writer process to stream a large file in chunks into the watched directory while daemon runs; assert processing does not start during writes and starts only after writer closes/completes.
